items.title: What actually causes autoimmune disease (and what does not)
items.excerpt: Your immune system isn't broken. It's responding to something. Here is the honest, research-backed map of what triggers it, why three things have to line up at once, and where to start looking when conventional medicine offers only suppression.
items.body:
Your immune system is not betraying you. It is doing its job in a body that has been asked to absorb more inputs than any human body in history was designed for, and at some point it began responding to one of them with too much force, for too long. That is autoimmunity in one sentence. The far more useful question is which input, and why now, and what is keeping it switched on.

TL;DR. Autoimmunity rises when three things meet: a genetic susceptibility, a leaky gut, and an environmental trigger. Take any one leg of the stool away and the immune attack quiets. This essay maps where to begin looking when conventional medicine offers only suppression.

The three-legged stool: genes, gut, trigger

The clearest framework comes from researcher Alessio Fasano, who has argued for years that autoimmunity requires three things to coincide: a genetic susceptibility, increased intestinal permeability (commonly called leaky gut), and an environmental trigger. Take any leg away and the stool falls. That is hopeful, because two of the three legs are addressable, even when the genes themselves are not. You can read his argument in plain English in his book Gut: The Inside Story of Our Body's Most Underrated Organ, and in his peer-reviewed work on zonulin, including this 2020 paper in F1000Research that synthesizes the model.

The genes load the gun. The trigger pulls the trigger. The leaky gut is the doorway that lets the trigger reach a part of the immune system it was never supposed to meet.

The triggers people miss

The list is longer than most people are told. Gluten and other gut-irritating proteins are well documented. So are infections, and the literature on Epstein Barr Virus and multiple sclerosis became impossible to ignore after the 2022 Harvard Science paper that tracked ten million U.S. military personnel over two decades. There is mold. There are heavy metals. There is chronic stress, the kind that keeps cortisol elevated for years, and which Donna Jackson Nakazawa traces with great care in her book The Autoimmune Epidemic. There are pesticides and the standard American diet. There is alcohol. There is fragmented sleep.

Most people with an autoimmune diagnosis do not have one trigger. They have several, layered over years.

Why suppression is not the same as healing

Conventional rheumatology and endocrinology are very good at calming a flaring immune system once it is already on fire. They have to be, because flares damage tissue. But suppression is not the same as removing the input that started the fire. If a gluten sensitivity in a genetically primed person opened the doorway in the first place, no biologic will close that door. It will just turn down the volume of the screaming on the other side of it.

This is not an argument against medication. It is an argument for doing both.

Where the work actually starts

If you are at the very beginning of this, three things are worth doing in order. First, get your antibodies tested so you know what you are tracking and what is improving. Second, address the gut, which is where the doorway lives. We covered that in leaky gut without the hype. Third, look at the inputs in your daily life, food, stress, sleep, infections, mold, with the assumption that one or two of them are still pulling the trigger. The AIP protocol is one of the cleaner ways to begin removing food triggers, and the work on the stress and the HPA axis is what addresses the input that almost no one mentions in a fifteen minute appointment.

The Autoimmune Reset is built around exactly this map. Every essay here exists to help one of the three legs of the stool become smaller, or to help you see which of the inputs in your life is still keeping the immune system in alarm.

items.title: Leaky gut, without the hype, without the dismissal
items.excerpt: The wellness internet oversold it. Most rheumatologists still call it pseudoscience. The truth, which Alessio Fasano has been quietly publishing since 2000, is that intestinal permeability is real, measurable, and central to autoimmunity. Here is what is actually known.
items.body:
For twenty years two camps have been talking past each other about the gut. The wellness internet treats leaky gut as the explanation for everything, from acne to anxiety to autoimmune flares. A lot of conventional rheumatology still rolls its eyes and calls the whole concept pseudoscience. Both camps are wrong, and the truth in the middle is genuinely useful for anyone with an autoimmune diagnosis.

TL;DR. Increased intestinal permeability is real, measurable, and central to autoimmunity. Removing what loosens the tight junctions and feeding what repairs them is the work that closes the doorway. This essay covers the evidence in plain English.

What leaky gut actually is

The technical name is increased intestinal permeability. Your small intestine is lined with a single layer of cells, sealed at their edges by tight-junction proteins. When those tight junctions loosen, contents of the gut that were never meant to cross the barrier, undigested food fragments, bacterial debris, lipopolysaccharide, slip through and meet the immune system on the other side. The immune system reacts. Over time, in a genetically susceptible person, that reaction can pivot into autoimmunity.

The protein that opens the tight junctions is called zonulin. It was identified by Alessio Fasano's lab in Boston in 2000. There is a peer-reviewed body of work on it, including a 2009 review in Physiological Reviews that anyone arguing leaky gut isn't real should be required to read.

What loosens the junctions

Several inputs are well documented. Gliadin, the protein in gluten, raises zonulin in essentially everyone, not only in people with celiac disease. Chronic stress raises it. So does alcohol, ibuprofen taken regularly, certain infections, and dysbiosis (an unbalanced gut bacterial community). Fasano's full case is laid out for general readers in Gut: The Inside Story of Our Body's Most Underrated Organ, and a more practical companion is Heal Your Gut by Cooking with Bone Broth.

How it becomes autoimmunity

This is where the doorway image gets serious. Once gut contents are crossing into circulation regularly, the immune system is mounting a low-grade response on a daily basis. In a person with the right (or wrong) HLA genes, that response can begin to mistake the body's own tissues for the foreign material it has been chasing. The thyroid in Hashimoto's, myelin in MS, joint synovium in rheumatoid arthritis. This is one of the documented mechanisms behind molecular mimicry.

What actually helps

Removing the inputs that loosen the junctions is the first move. For most autoimmune patients that means a real trial of gluten removal, often expanded into the broader AIP protocol. Then the layer of repair: bone broth, well-cooked vegetables, fermented foods if tolerated, glutamine in some cases, omega-3s, and the slower repair work of addressing chronic stress, which is the single most underrated driver of intestinal permeability we cover at The Autoimmune Reset.

None of this is a cure. It is a closing of the doorway, so that genetics and triggers no longer have a path to meet.

items.title: An honest guide to the autoimmune protocol (AIP)
items.excerpt: The AIP is not a forever diet. It is a thirty-to-ninety-day diagnostic, a way of asking the body, in food, what it has been quietly inflamed by. Done right it is one of the most powerful elimination diets in clinical use. Done wrong it becomes another orthorexic spiral.
items.body:
The autoimmune protocol gets used badly more often than it gets used well. People hear that it can quiet a flare, they go on it indefinitely, they never reintroduce, and they end up with a smaller and smaller list of foods they trust. That is not the protocol Sarah Ballantyne wrote, and it is not how it should be used. Done right, AIP is a short, intentional diagnostic that gives you back information your body has been trying to send you for years.

TL;DR. The autoimmune protocol is a thirty- to ninety-day diagnostic, not a forever diet. Done right, it gives the immune system a quiet window and gives you back information your body has been trying to send for years.

What AIP actually is

For thirty to ninety days you remove the foods most commonly implicated in autoimmune flares: gluten, dairy, eggs, soy, all grains, all legumes (yes, peanuts), nightshades (tomato, potato, pepper, eggplant), nuts and seeds, refined sugar, alcohol, coffee, and certain food additives. You eat well-cooked vegetables, quality animal protein, fish, bone broth, fermented foods if tolerated, and a generous amount of healthy fats. Then, and this is the part most people skip, you reintroduce foods one at a time and watch.

The full framework is in Sarah Ballantyne's The Paleo Approach, the book that turned AIP from a clinical idea into something a regular person could actually do. The cookbook companion The Healing Kitchen is what most people actually cook from, and it is what we recommend to readers who tell us they are starting next week.

Why it works when it works

AIP is doing two things at once. It is removing the foods most likely to keep intestinal permeability open in someone who is autoimmune. And it is starving the version of the immune response that has been quietly running on background inflammation. Most people who finish a clean elimination phase notice fewer flares, less brain fog, less joint pain, and better sleep. There is a reason a small but growing number of functional-medicine clinicians open with AIP for new autoimmune patients.

What goes wrong

Three things. First, people stay in elimination forever, which slowly narrows the diet, weakens the gut microbiome, and turns into orthorexia. Second, people skip the reintroduction phase, which is where the actual diagnostic information lives, without it you never learn which foods were the culprits. Third, people use AIP without addressing chronic stress, which is one of the strongest drivers of leaky gut and which no diet on earth can override.

How to do it well

Plan the elimination phase as a thirty- or sixty-day window with a clear end date. Cook in batches. Keep a notebook. Reintroduce in the order Ballantyne recommends, one food at a time, every three to five days. Notice symptoms for seventy-two hours after each. Build a list of trusted foods and a list of suspect ones, and let the body tell you which is which. The Autoimmune Reset will keep adding to its library of practical guides on the trickier reintroductions, in particular eggs, nuts, and nightshades, which trip up the most people.

items.title: The stress no one in the appointment ever asks about
items.excerpt: The single most underrated driver of autoimmune flares is chronic activation of the HPA axis. It opens the gut, it shifts immune polarization, it fragments sleep. And it is the one input that almost no rheumatology appointment will name out loud.
items.body:
If you sit through a fifteen-minute rheumatology appointment, you will be asked about your medications, your symptoms, your labs, and the affected joints. You will almost never be asked how you sleep, what your week has felt like, what happened to you when you were eight, or whether you currently feel safe in your own life. That is not the rheumatologist's fault. It is the system's fault. But the consequence is that the single most underrated driver of autoimmune disease almost never makes it into the conversation.

TL;DR. Chronic activation of the HPA axis loosens the gut, polarizes the immune system, and fragments sleep. It is the single most under-addressed input in the autoimmune appointment, and the most actionable one at home.

What the HPA axis is, in plain English

The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis is the body's main stress response. It runs on cortisol and adrenaline, and it is supposed to spike, do its job, and shut off. In modern life it does not shut off. It runs at low simmer for years. Chronic activation of that axis does several things at once. It loosens the tight junctions in the gut, raising intestinal permeability. It shifts immune polarization toward Th17 dominance, which is implicated in nearly every autoimmune condition. It suppresses the secretory IgA in the gut, which is the immune system's first line of defense against pathogens. And it fragments slow-wave sleep, which is when most repair happens.

The evidence is not soft

This is not woo. The literature linking adverse childhood experiences to lifetime autoimmune risk is large and growing. Vincent Felitti's original ACE study, and its many replications, found a dose-response relationship between childhood trauma and adult autoimmune diagnosis. Donna Jackson Nakazawa walks through this work for general readers in Childhood Disrupted, a book we have linked to in the trauma essay for the same reason.

For the nervous system side, Stephen Porges's polyvagal theory is the cleanest map of how chronic threat physiology becomes chronic illness physiology. The Pocket Guide to Polyvagal Theory is the short version. Bessel van der Kolk's The Body Keeps the Score is the long, beautifully written version.

What actually moves the dial

Three categories of practice are well-supported. Slow, paced breathing, particularly anything that lengthens the exhale, increases vagal tone and lowers cortisol within minutes, and across weeks it raises heart rate variability. Daily nervous-system regulation practices: cold water on the face, humming, gentle yoga, walking outside in green light. And the slower, deeper work of trauma therapy in cases where chronic threat physiology is rooted in early life experiences. EMDR, Somatic Experiencing, IFS, the modalities that respect the body, not just the story.

You do not have to do all of this. But you cannot fix autoimmune disease while leaving the HPA axis on simmer. We will keep covering the practical, fifteen-minutes-a-day version of this work in the library, because the body is not separate from the life around it. Related reading from The Autoimmune Reset library: leaky gut.

items.title: Hashimoto's, the thyroid, and the autoimmune mosaic
items.excerpt: Hashimoto's is the most common autoimmune disease in the developed world, and one of the most undertreated. Conventional care manages the thyroid output. Root-cause work asks why the immune system is attacking the thyroid in the first place.
items.body:
Hashimoto's thyroiditis is the most common autoimmune disease in the developed world. It is also one of the most undertreated, in the sense that the standard of care addresses the symptom, low thyroid hormone output, while almost never addressing the underlying autoimmune attack on the thyroid itself. If you are tired, cold, foggy, dropping hair, gaining weight without explanation, and your TSH is high, that is the moment the system tends to hand you levothyroxine and call the visit done. Hormone replacement is appropriate, and we are not arguing against it. But it is not the whole picture.

TL;DR. The disease is the antibody attack, not just the symptom. Replacing the missing hormone is necessary; addressing why the immune system is attacking the gland is what slows the progression.

The attack is the disease

Hashimoto's is defined by elevated thyroid antibodies, in particular thyroid peroxidase antibodies (TPO Ab) and thyroglobulin antibodies (TG Ab). Those antibodies are the immune system's evidence that it is attacking thyroid tissue. The thyroid output drops because the gland is being damaged. Replacing the hormone is treating the smoke. The fire is the antibody titer. Lower the titer and you slow or stop the destruction.

Datis Kharrazian wrote the book that made this case to a wider audience: Why Do I Still Have Thyroid Symptoms? When My Lab Tests Are Normal. Izabella Wentz's Hashimoto's Protocol is the practical companion many readers actually work through.

What drives the attack

The same map as the rest of autoimmunity, with thyroid-specific notes. Gluten cross-reacts with thyroid tissue in a fraction of patients (the gliadin/transglutaminase story is published and increasingly taken seriously). Iodine excess can worsen Hashimoto's in some people, while iodine deficiency can also play a role; this is one of the cases where DIY supplementation backfires often. Selenium deficiency is well-documented as a contributor; selenium replacement at modest doses (around 200 mcg) lowers TPO antibodies in trials. Chronic stress and elevated cortisol slow the conversion of T4 to active T3. EBV reactivation appears to be a trigger in a meaningful subset of cases, which connects this directly to our piece on Epstein-Barr and autoimmunity.

The tests most appointments skip

A standard panel is often only TSH. A useful Hashimoto's panel includes TSH, free T4, free T3, reverse T3, TPO antibodies, and thyroglobulin antibodies. Without antibodies you cannot tell whether you have Hashimoto's at all, which is why up to a third of cases get missed for years. Bring this list to your appointment, or to a functional-medicine practitioner who will run it without negotiation.

The actual plan

It looks a lot like the autoimmune plan we keep coming back to. Address the gut. Run a real trial of gluten removal, often as part of the broader AIP protocol. Replace selenium if low. Treat infections if they show up. Take the HPA axis seriously. Track the antibodies, not just the TSH, and let the antibody trend be the scoreboard. The Autoimmune Reset will keep returning to Hashimoto's specifically, because more readers find this site through that diagnosis than any other.

items.title: Molecular mimicry: why your immune system mistakes you for something foreign
items.excerpt: Molecular mimicry is one of the cleanest mechanistic explanations for how autoimmunity gets started, and one of the least well explained outside of immunology textbooks. Here it is in plain English, with the implications that actually matter for treatment.
items.body:
The idea behind molecular mimicry is older than the term. Tom O'Bryan and Aristo Vojdani have probably done more than anyone to drag it from immunology journals into the language patients can use. The mechanism is simple to describe and important to understand, because it changes how you think about food sensitivities, infections, and what an autoimmune diagnosis actually is.

TL;DR. Autoimmunity rises when three things meet: a genetic susceptibility, a leaky gut, and an environmental trigger. Take any one leg of the stool away and the immune attack quiets. This essay maps where to begin looking when conventional medicine offers only suppression.

The basic idea

Your immune system recognizes things by their shape. Specifically, it recognizes peptide sequences, short chains of amino acids, and it makes antibodies and trains T-cells to attack those shapes. Most of the time the shapes it learns to attack belong to genuine threats: viruses, bacteria, parasites. The problem is that some of those foreign shapes happen to look very much like shapes on your own tissues. That structural similarity is what we call molecular mimicry.

Once the immune system has been trained to attack the foreign shape, it can begin attacking the lookalike shape on your tissue too. That is autoimmunity in mechanistic form.

The best-known examples

Streptococcus and rheumatic heart disease, where strep antigens look like cardiac muscle. Epstein-Barr Virus and multiple sclerosis, formalized in the 2022 Harvard Science paper and the EBNA-1 / GlialCAM mimicry story. Gluten (specifically the gliadin fraction) and the enzyme transglutaminase, which is the central mimic in celiac disease and which Aristo Vojdani's lab has documented as cross-reactive with thyroid, brain, and other tissues. There is a good lay-audience treatment of this work in The Autoimmune Fix by Tom O'Bryan.

Why this matters for the plan

Molecular mimicry is one of the strongest arguments for taking food sensitivities seriously even when they are not classical IgE allergies. If a food protein looks structurally like a thyroid protein, eating it daily for years can train the immune system to keep attacking the thyroid even when no food is in the system. That is part of why AIP-style elimination can quiet thyroid antibodies even when no overt food allergy was suspected. It is also part of why we keep linking infections like Epstein-Barr to autoimmune onset, and why the underlying picture, the one that runs through the entire library, is the same: identify what your immune system has been training on, and remove it. Related reading from The Autoimmune Reset library: leaky gut.

items.title: Epstein-Barr Virus and autoimmunity: the connection that became impossible to ignore
items.excerpt: For decades clinicians suspected a link between EBV and autoimmune disease. In 2022, a study of ten million U.S. military personnel made the link nearly inarguable, at least for multiple sclerosis. The implications for the rest of autoimmunity are still being worked out.
items.body:
For thirty years a quiet body of literature suggested Epstein-Barr Virus might be doing something to the immune systems of the people it infects in childhood and then sits inside for the rest of their lives. In 2022 that quiet literature became loud. A team led by Alberto Ascherio at Harvard published a study in Science that followed ten million U.S. military service members over two decades and found that EBV infection raised the risk of multiple sclerosis thirty-two-fold. That is not a soft signal. The full paper is here: Longitudinal analysis reveals high prevalence of Epstein-Barr Virus associated with multiple sclerosis.

TL;DR. Autoimmunity rises when three things meet: a genetic susceptibility, a leaky gut, and an environmental trigger. Take any one leg of the stool away and the immune attack quiets. This essay maps where to begin looking when conventional medicine offers only suppression.

What EBV is doing inside the body

EBV infects more than ninety-five percent of adults worldwide. Most people never get visibly sick from it, or get a single bout of mononucleosis in adolescence and move on. The virus then takes up residence in B-cells and is held in check by a competent immune system. The trouble starts when the immune system loses that grip, under chronic stress, after a second infection, after years of gut permeability driving immune dysregulation. Reactivated EBV can drive inflammation, and through molecular mimicry can train the immune system to attack tissues that look structurally similar to viral proteins.

Beyond MS

The cleanest evidence is in MS. There is also strong evidence in lupus and a meaningful signal in Hashimoto's, rheumatoid arthritis, and Sjogren's. Epidemiologically, infectious mononucleosis in adolescence consistently shows up as a risk factor for later autoimmune diagnosis. It does not mean EBV is the cause of all autoimmunity. It means it is one of the most common triggers, and if you are in a flare and have not asked about EBV reactivation, you are skipping a question worth asking.

What helps

This part is humbler. There is no antiviral that clears EBV. The immune system has to keep it in check. So the work is the work that runs through this site: take the load off the immune system by closing the gut, calming the HPA axis, sleeping enough to allow the night-time clearance work to happen, and eating in a way that does not keep low-grade inflammation switched on (often the AIP framework). Some functional clinicians use targeted antivirals, monolaurin, or higher-dose vitamin C in active reactivation; that work belongs with a clinician who knows your case. The Autoimmune Reset will keep tracking the EBV literature, because for many readers it has been the missing piece.

items.title: The Wahls Protocol: mitochondria, greens, and a clinician who got out of her wheelchair
items.excerpt: Terry Wahls had progressive multiple sclerosis. She was in a tilt-recline wheelchair. Then she rebuilt her diet around the requirements of mitochondrial function, and over a few years she walked again. Her protocol is one of the most rigorous nutrient-density diets in autoimmune care.
items.body:
Terry Wahls was a clinical professor of medicine at the University of Iowa when her secondary progressive multiple sclerosis put her in a tilt-recline wheelchair. She had access to the best biologics that existed. They were not stopping the decline. So she did what physician-patients sometimes do when the standard of care has run out: she went to PubMed and rebuilt her diet from first principles, around the question of what the mitochondria, the energy factories inside cells, actually need to function.

TL;DR. The autoimmune protocol is a thirty- to ninety-day diagnostic, not a forever diet. Done right, it gives the immune system a quiet window and gives you back information your body has been trying to send for years.

What she found

Mitochondria need a long list of cofactors that the modern diet often does not deliver in adequate amounts: B-vitamins, sulfur, omega-3s, iodine, and a wide spectrum of plant phytonutrients. She designed an eating pattern around hitting all of those at once, every day. Nine cups of vegetables (three leafy greens, three deeply colored, three sulfur-rich), high-quality animal protein especially organ meats, wild fish, seaweed for iodine, and elimination of gluten, dairy, and processed foods. She wrote it up for general readers in The Wahls Protocol, with a cookbook companion at The Wahls Protocol Cooking for Life.

The evidence is no longer anecdote

Wahls and her team have run formal trials at the University of Iowa since. A multi-year randomized study of the Wahls diet versus a low-saturated-fat diet in MS patients showed reductions in fatigue and improvements in quality of life. There is more in the pipeline. The bottom line is that this is no longer just one woman's story. It is a clinically tested nutrient-density protocol that overlaps significantly with the AIP framework we already cover.

How it fits with the rest of the picture

The Wahls Protocol is not different from the autoimmune story we tell at The Autoimmune Reset; it is a particularly nutrient-dense expression of it. It removes the foods most likely to drive intestinal permeability. It floods the system with the cofactors mitochondria need to make ATP. And in pairing those two with the rest of the work, sleep, stress, infections, it gives the immune system back the bandwidth to stop attacking. Wahls herself is clear that diet is necessary and not sufficient. The whole picture is the picture. The diet is the foundation underneath everything else. Related reading from The Autoimmune Reset library: leaky gut.

items.title: Childhood trauma and autoimmunity: what the ACE studies have been quietly telling us
items.excerpt: The connection between childhood adversity and adult autoimmune disease is one of the strongest, most replicated findings in the trauma literature. It is also one of the least likely to come up in your appointment. Here is what the evidence actually says, and what to do with it.
items.body:
The Adverse Childhood Experiences study, which Vincent Felitti and Robert Anda began at Kaiser in the late 1990s, is one of the most replicated public-health findings of the past three decades. It found a clean dose-response relationship between the number of types of adversity a child is exposed to before age eighteen and their risk of nearly every adult chronic illness, including autoimmune disease. That is not the same as saying childhood adversity caused your autoimmunity. It means it loaded the dice. Donna Jackson Nakazawa walks through the entire literature in Childhood Disrupted, and Bessel van der Kolk's The Body Keeps the Score is the longer companion that explains the mechanisms in the body.

TL;DR. Childhood adversity is one of the strongest, most replicated risk factors for adult autoimmune disease. Acknowledging that does not assign blame; it points to layers of repair that the food and supplement plan alone will not reach.

How adversity becomes autoimmunity in the body

Chronic, unpredictable threat in childhood keeps the HPA axis activated during the years the brain and immune system are still developing. The result is a nervous system that is calibrated to expect threat and an immune system that has been trained alongside it. Decades later, that nervous system is still running threat physiology in a body that no longer needs it. Cortisol stays elevated. Vagal tone stays low. Sleep is fragmented. The gut barrier is loose. The immune system is on simmer for years, and then a trigger arrives, an infection, a pregnancy, a sustained period of life stress, and the immune response pivots into autoimmunity.

This is the mechanism Gabor Maté describes in his clinical work, and the mechanism Nakazawa traces in patient after patient. It is not abstract.

What this does not mean

It does not mean autoimmunity is your fault, or your parents' fault. It does not mean trauma work alone will cure rheumatoid arthritis or Hashimoto's. And it does not give anyone permission to skip medication, gut work, or the rest of the picture we cover in the library. What it means is that there is a real, biological reason the trauma layer keeps appearing in autoimmune recovery stories, and a real, evidence-based reason to take it seriously.

What actually helps

The therapies with the best evidence for somatic, nervous-system-rooted trauma are not classical talk therapy alone. They are EMDR, Somatic Experiencing, Internal Family Systems, and certain kinds of trauma-informed yoga. They are slow. They cost money. And they are, for many readers, the missing piece in why their AIP and gut work stalled at sixty percent better. The Autoimmune Reset will keep treating this layer with the seriousness it deserves, because the body is not separate from the life that has lived inside it.

items.title: What a functional-medicine appointment actually looks like (and when it is worth the money)
items.excerpt: Functional medicine has a real range. At its best it is the careful, root-cause work conventional medicine no longer has time for. At its worst it is expensive supplement sales. Here is how to tell the difference, and how to use it well.
items.body:
Functional medicine has become the umbrella under which a lot of root-cause autoimmune work happens in the United States. It is also wildly uneven. At its best, a functional-medicine appointment is the ninety-minute, lab-driven, history-taking, root-cause conversation conventional rheumatology no longer has time for. At its worst, it is a supplement subscription dressed up in white-coat language. Both versions exist. It is worth knowing how to tell them apart before you spend the money, because the good version is one of the most useful tools an autoimmune patient has, and the bad version is expensive without delivering.

TL;DR. At its best, functional medicine is the lab-driven, root-cause appointment conventional rheumatology no longer has time for. At its worst, it is supplement sales. This essay covers how to tell the difference before you spend the money.

What the good version actually looks like

An initial visit lasts at least sixty minutes, often ninety. The clinician takes a real timeline of your life, not just the timeline of your symptoms. They run labs that almost no rheumatology office runs by default: comprehensive thyroid antibodies, GI MAP or similar gut microbiome testing, organic acids, mycotoxins if mold exposure is suspected, EBV reactivation panels, often nutrient panels for selenium, zinc, vitamin D, B12, and ferritin. They look at your existing medications and do not ask you to stop them. They build a plan that overlaps significantly with what we cover in the library: address the gut, calm the HPA axis, treat infections, fix nutrient gaps, and use food (often AIP-style) as the foundation. Susan Blum's The Immune System Recovery Plan is one of the cleanest patient-facing maps of how this work proceeds.

What the bad version looks like

You walk in, you fill out a form, the clinician orders an enormous lab panel without listening to your story, and you walk out with a printout that pushes a long list of branded supplements you can only buy through their dispensary. There is no follow-up plan beyond reordering the supplements. The labs are not interpreted in the context of your life. That is not functional medicine. That is sales.

How to find the good version

The Institute for Functional Medicine has a directory of certified practitioners, which is a reasonable starting point but not a guarantee. Better signals: ask whether the clinician is willing to work with your existing rheumatologist or endocrinologist, whether they expect you to stay on your medications during gut and nutrient work, whether their plan includes lifestyle and trauma-informed work alongside supplements, and whether they will share lab results in writing rather than only verbally. Tom O'Bryan's The Autoimmune Fix is a useful read before you book the first appointment, because it gives you the vocabulary to ask the questions that filter the good clinician from the salesperson.

What it pairs with

Functional medicine works best as the lab-and-clinician layer on top of the work you can do at home: food, gut, nervous system, sleep. Without that home layer, no panel of labs will get you well. With that layer, the right clinician can short-cut years of guessing. The Autoimmune Reset will keep adding to the practical, non-romantic guides that help readers know which version of this they have walked into.
